# Avinash Sangle — Claude Code & AI Expert from Pune

> AI Automation Engineer and Claude Code expert based in Pune, India. Building AI-powered developer tools, MCP servers, and DevOps automation. Specializes in Claude Code workflows, Model Context Protocol, and agentic engineering.

## Author
- Website: https://avinashsangle.com
- LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/avinashsangle
- Twitter/X: https://x.com/avi_sangle
- GitHub: https://github.com/avisangle
- Location: Pune, Maharashtra, India
- Expertise: Claude Code, AI Automation, Model Context Protocol, DevOps, Generative AI

## Blog Posts
- [How to Run GLM-5.2 Locally for AI Coding (2026 Guide)](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/glm-5-2-local-coding-guide): Practitioner guide to running GLM-5.2 locally for coding. GLM-5.2 is Z.ai's (Zhipu AI) open-weight Mixture-of-Experts model (744B total / ~40B active params, 1M-token context, 128K output, MIT license), shipped June 13 2026 to Coding Plan subscribers with open weights (BF16 + FP8) on June 16. It is the #1 open-weight model on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1 (score 51, +11 over GLM-5.1's 40, NOT the 41-point jump early coverage claimed), behind only Claude Opus 4.8 (56) and GPT-5.5 (55). Coding benchmarks: SWE-bench Pro 62.1 (official, beats GPT-5.5's 58.6), FrontierSWE 74.4% (near-ties Opus 4.8's 75.1), Terminal-Bench 2.1 81.0 (trails Opus 85 / GPT-5.5 84); reported ~77.8% SWE-bench Verified (secondary source). Known weakness: verbosity ~42K tokens/task vs GPT-5.5-high's ~10K. Hosted API ~$1.40/$4.40 per 1M tokens (~1/6th of GPT-5.5/Opus). Local story is Unsloth Dynamic GGUFs: key rule is MoE saves compute not memory so all 744B weights must sit in RAM+VRAM. Quant table: UD-IQ1_S 217GB (~76% top-1), UD-IQ2_M 239GB (~82%, fits 256GB Mac), UD-Q4_K_XL 467GB ("mostly lossless", coding floor), Q8_0 801GB (safe target), BF16 1.51TB. Practitioner caveat: heavy quants below Q4 emit malformed tool calls and loops; use Q4+ for coding, Q8 if you can. Hardware tiers: 2-bit needs ~245GB (256GB Mac Studio M3/M4 Ultra, or 24GB GPU + 256GB RAM with MoE offload); Q4-Q8 needs 466-820GB (multi-GPU server or high-RAM EPYC/Threadripper). Throughput tracks memory bandwidth: ~1 tok/s CPU-only, ~6 tok/s dual RTX 3090 + offload, vs 80-200 tok/s cloud. Runtime: llama.cpp (primary, with build + hf download + sampling temp 1.0/top_p 0.95/min_p 0.01 + KV-cache quant), LM Studio (Mac GUI), vLLM FP8 (datacenter), Ollama is cloud-only (glm-5.2:cloud, no local tag). Coding-agent integration via OpenAI-compatible endpoint (llama serve / llama-server at localhost:8080/v1): Aider recipe (OPENAI_API_BASE + openai/GLM-5.2), Cline/Continue OpenAI-compatible provider. Honest verdict: run locally for privacy/offline/high-volume batch with 256GB+ hardware; stay on cloud for interactive speed and hard multi-file reasoning, where the hosted GLM-5.2 API is often the saner middle ground. Links to qwen-code-getting-started, gemma-4-models-guide, apple-core-ai-on-device-inference-guide, and claude-code-cost-tracking as an open-weight + local-inference cluster.
- [Claude Tag for Engineering Teams: A Practical Setup Guide](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-tag-engineering-teams-guide): Practitioner's guide to setting up Claude Tag, Anthropic's persistent AI Slack teammate launched June 23, 2026 (runs on Opus 4.8, beta for Claude Enterprise and Team). Unlike the old request-response Claude in Slack app, Claude Tag is one shared @Claude identity per channel with admin-configured access, persistent channel-scoped memory, ambient mode, and async multi-stage task execution; it replaces the old app under the same Slack app with nothing to reinstall (65% of Anthropic's own product team's code is created by their internal version). Setup is admin-led: an organization Owner provisions it at claude.ai/admin-settings/claude-tag in four steps (create the identity, attach an access bundle of credentials and repos, scope it to channels/workspaces, test). Key principle: access follows the channel, not the user, and users cannot override it; invite Claude with /invite @Claude. Covers connecting developer tools via MCP servers (300+ integrations: GitHub, Sentry, Linear, PostgreSQL, plus custom internal servers like a Jenkins or CI MCP), a channel-to-tools mapping (incidents->Sentry/PagerDuty, PRs->GitHub write, planning->Linear, CI->custom MCP), least-privilege bundles per channel, and the prompt-injection caveat (only use in trusted channels). Explains ambient mode (proactively reads channel messages to surface info; enable on ops/incident channels, keep off where sensitive content mixes), token cost control (consumption-based billing, Owner sets a hard spend cap, per-channel breakdown at claude.ai/admin-settings/usage/claude-in-slack, intro usage credit through Sept 1 2026, channel work bills the org while DMs bill the user seat), routing modes (Code only vs Code + Chat), and a comparison of Claude Tag (async Slack coordination layer, opens PRs via Claude Code) vs terminal Claude Code (interactive deep-work) vs Microsoft Copilot in Teams (GitHub-platform anchored). Links to claude-managed-agents, claude-code-cost-tracking, mcp-code-execution-pattern, hardening-ai-agents-cicd-prompt-injection, and the jenkins-mcp and method-crm-mcp projects.
- [Apple Core AI: Run Open-Weight Models On-Device for Free](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/apple-core-ai-on-device-inference-guide): Practitioner's guide to Apple Core AI, the on-device inference framework announced at WWDC 2026 (June 8-9). Core AI is the generative-AI successor to Core ML: a memory-safe Swift API for running LLMs entirely on Apple Silicon (iPhone, iPad, Mac, Vision Pro) across CPU/GPU/Neural Engine, with ahead-of-time compilation, zero-copy data paths, stateful execution, and inference-memory control. Key advantage is zero token cost and zero server dependency (data never leaves device), making it a real cost lever for high-volume, privacy-sensitive, offline, or latency-critical workloads. Apple ships curated pre-converted open-weight models (Qwen, Mistral, SAM3) as Swift packages in apple/coreai-models. Covers the coreai-torch PyTorch conversion pipeline (three steps: torch.export.export, ep.run_decompositions(get_decomp_table()), TorchConverter().add_exported_program(ep).to_coreai() then .optimize()), the add_exported_program vs add_pytorch_module decision table, custom ops via register_torch_lowering and TorchMetalKernel, and the composite-ops library (RoPE, RMSNorm, SDPA, attention). Shows the Swift API: a CoreAILanguageModel plugs into the same FoundationModels LanguageModelSession (session.respond) with streaming and @Generable structured output, and ImageSegmenter for SAM3. Covers quantization/palettization for mobile via apple/coreai-optimization, Xcode graph inspection plus the Core AI Debugger, and an honest on-device-vs-cloud decision matrix (on-device for cost/privacy/offline/latency on smaller models; cloud like Claude or Gemini for frontier reasoning, huge context, cross-platform reach). Links to the Gemma 4 models guide, Qwen Code getting-started, and Claude Code cost-tracking posts as an open-weight + cost cluster.
- [LiteLLM CVE-2026-42271: Patch, Rotate, and Harden the RCE](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/litellm-mcp-exploit-response-guide): Incident-response runbook for the LiteLLM MCP exploit chain. CVE-2026-42271 (command injection, CVSS 8.7) lives in LiteLLM's MCP server test endpoints (/mcp-rest/test/connection and /mcp-rest/test/tools/list) which accepted a full stdio server config (command, args, env) and spawned it as an unsandboxed subprocess on the proxy host. Chained with CVE-2026-48710 (Starlette "BadHost" host-header parsing bypass, CVSS 6.5, affects Starlette 0.8.3-1.0.0), the auth requirement disappears and the result is unauthenticated remote code execution. CISA added CVE-2026-42271 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on June 9 2026 citing active exploitation. Affected: LiteLLM 1.74.2 through 1.83.6. Fixed: LiteLLM 1.83.7 plus Starlette 1.0.1 (both must be upgraded; patching LiteLLM alone leaves the authenticated injection open). Covers detection (version check, probing whether /mcp-rest/test/ routes are reachable, grepping access logs for the routes and odd Host headers), the two-package upgrade path with pip/requirements.txt/Dockerfile examples and a re-probe to verify, the credential-rotation step most coverage skips (rotate all provider keys, the LITELLM_MASTER_KEY, virtual/team keys, and DB credentials since RCE exposes the environment; partial rotation fails because a leaked master key mints new virtual keys), hardening (network allowlist on admin+MCP routes, unprivileged read-only container with dropped capabilities, least-privilege keys, removing/sandboxing config-preview endpoints), and an honest "should you still trust LiteLLM" verdict after two critical 2026 incidents (March supply-chain backdoor + June RCE). Links to the MCP code execution pattern and CI/CD prompt-injection hardening posts as a security cluster.
- [Claude Code Fable 5: Model Routing, Fallbacks, Cost Control](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-code-fable-5-model-routing): Practitioner guide to deploying Claude Fable 5 (released June 9 2026, $10/$50 per 1M tokens, exactly 2x Opus 4.8's $5/$25, free on Pro/Max/Team through June 22 2026, requires Claude Code v2.1.170+) in real Claude Code sessions. Distinguishes the two separate fallback systems everyone conflates: fallbackModel availability chains (shipped v2.1.166, up to 3 backups after dedup, per-turn switch only, set via --fallback-model flag or settings array, never fires on auth/billing/rate-limit errors, elements outside availableModels silently dropped) versus the sticky safety-classifier fallback (cyber + bio content reroutes to Opus 4.8 and the session stays there until /model fable). Documents the first-request gotcha: workspace context (CLAUDE.md, git status, directory names) can trip the classifier before you type anything; debug with claude --safe-mode, or turn off "switch models when a message is flagged" in /config to pause-and-choose instead. Covers fallback billing (classifier-blocked input not billed; Fable-to-Opus fallback input at cache-read rate, 10% of base), a task-routing table (Fable 5 for root-cause debugging, multi-file refactors, long autonomous runs; Opus 4.8 daily driver and CI security scanning since non-interactive flagged requests refuse rather than fall back; Sonnet 4.6 for scoped fixes), effort interaction (Fable default high, thinking cannot be disabled), and Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry setup (ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_FABLE_MODEL + ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_OPUS_MODEL required for auto-fallback; 30-day retention requirement; unavailable under ZDR).
- [Claude Code Dynamic Workflows: When They're Worth the Cost](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-code-dynamic-workflows-guide): Practitioner guide to Claude Code dynamic workflows (shipped May 28 2026 with Opus 4.8, research preview, requires v2.1.154+, all paid plans with Pro opt-in via /config). Explains what a dynamic workflow is (a JavaScript orchestration script Claude writes; runtime runs it in the background; intermediate results live in script variables so only the final answer hits Claude's context; primitives agent()/parallel()/pipeline()/phase()). Covers the workflow-vs-single-session decision rule (who holds the plan; subagents/skills keep results in context, workflows move loop + branching + results into a script that scales to hundreds of agents; Bun Zig-to-Rust port proof point: 750K lines, 11 days, 99.8% tests). Models the cost question no day-one guide answers: no fixed token rate, each agent pays its own context overhead at the session model's rate, a 500-agent audit can shift the bill by an order of magnitude, with an illustrative ~850K-token / 5-7x worked estimate and model-routing controls. Includes a step-by-step first run (/deep-research bundled workflow, the `workflow` keyword trigger, approval prompt with View raw script and Ctrl+G, save with `s`), prompt-scoping tactics to bound subagent count (16 concurrent / 1,000 total caps as a runaway backstop not a budget), an honest ultracode warning (xhigh effort + automatic orchestration, per-session, skips the Auto-mode launch prompt), a dynamic-workflows-vs-/ultrareview comparison for audits, and monitoring/pause/resume via /workflows (cached results on resume, but resume only works within the same session). Disable via /config, disableWorkflows, or CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_WORKFLOWS=1.
- [Persistent Memory for AI Coding Agents Beyond CLAUDE.md](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/persistent-memory-ai-coding-agents): Three-tier playbook for persistent memory across Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini CLI sessions. Tier 1 covers CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md and hierarchical loading. Tier 2 compares the leading MCP memory servers head-to-head with current star counts and benchmarks (agentmemory 19.5K stars, 95.2% R@5 on LongMemEval-S, ~92% context reduction, SQLite + BM25 + vector + KG, 12 auto-capture hooks, dashboard on :3113; claude-mem 79.5K stars, SQLite + FTS5 + Chroma, ~10x token savings, plugin marketplace install, Postgres + BullMQ server-beta for teams; mcp-memory-service with knowledge-graph entities/relations/observations and LangGraph/CrewAI/AutoGen integrations). Tier 3 covers Anthropic's Memory tool (memory_20250818 with /memories view/create/str_replace/insert/delete/rename commands, client-side storage, 84% token savings paired with the context-management-2025-06-27 context-editing beta header), Dreaming (research preview shipped May 6-7 2026 at Code with Claude SF, scheduled cross-session memory curation, Harvey ~6x task-completion lift), Memory for Managed Agents (public beta April 23 2026, per-write audit logs, cross-agent sharing), and Cloudflare Agent Memory (private beta April 17 2026). Includes a five-minute Claude Code install walkthrough using agentmemory, a five-row decision matrix by team size and runtime, and four trade-off sections (privacy + path traversal, token cost with concrete numbers, staleness, cross-agent leakage). Clarifies the most common confusion: the Memory tool is an API capability for SDK-built agents, not a Claude Code CLI feature.
- [Qwen Code CLI: Getting Started Guide for AI Coding 2026](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/qwen-code-getting-started): Practitioner getting-started guide for Qwen Code, QwenLM's open-source terminal AI agent built on Qwen3-Coder (256K native context, ~1M with YaRN). Covers npm install on macOS/Linux/Windows, the post-2026-04-15 OAuth shutdown and three API-key paths (Alibaba Cloud Model Studio DashScope, OpenRouter/Together/Fireworks OpenAI-compatible providers, self-hosted vLLM/Ollama with open Qwen3-Coder weights), ~/.qwen/settings.json config blocks, first-run TUI walkthrough, model variants (qwen3-coder-plus, qwen3-coder-flash, OpenRouter alias, 7B-Instruct self-host), YaRN latency/recall tradeoff past 400K tokens, a non-interactive `qwen -p` GitHub Actions PR-summary workflow, side-by-side comparison vs Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6 subscription) and the deprecating Gemini CLI (June 18 2026 shutdown), and common error fixes (401 OAuth, model not found, PATH issues).
- [Gemini 3.5 Flash for Agentic Coding: A Claude Coder's Guide](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/gemini-3-5-flash-agentic-coding-guide): Practitioner routing guide for Gemini 3.5 Flash (GA May 19 2026) written for Claude Code users deciding when to route subtasks to Google. Covers benchmarks (76.2% Terminal-Bench 2.1, 83.6% MCP Atlas beating Claude Opus 4.7 by 4.5 pts, GDPval-AA 1656 Elo, 55.1% SWE-Bench Pro; -7.6 pts 128k retrieval, -5 pts ARC-AGI-2), the cost-per-task truth ($1.50/$9/$0.15 per 1M tokens; Simon Willison cites $1,551.60 vs $892.28 on Artificial Analysis vs Gemini 3.1 Pro; NxCode reports 9x eval cost vs Gemini 3 Flash; GitHub Copilot 14x premium multiplier), the thinking_level default trap (silent drop from high to medium; use low for agentic coding, high for reasoning; temperature/top_p/top_k silently ignored), a six-row routing matrix (Sonnet 4.6 for repo edits, Flash for MCP fan-out, Opus 4.7 or GPT-5.5 for ARC-style reasoning, 3.1 Pro or 1M-ctx Sonnet for long context), three routing mechanisms (OpenRouter, custom MCP server, Antigravity CLI), a working 40-line Python MCP agent using google-genai with thinking_level=low, the four surfaces (raw API, Antigravity, Copilot, OpenRouter), and seven honest limitations (no Computer Use, January 2025 cutoff, text-only output, thought-token inflation 30-50%, 503 TPU hiccups in week one).
- [Gemini CLI to Antigravity CLI: Migration Guide & Alternatives](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/gemini-cli-to-antigravity-cli-guide): Practitioner decision guide for the June 18, 2026 Gemini CLI shutdown. Covers who is affected (free, Google AI Pro $19.99/mo, Google AI Ultra $249.99/mo cut off; Gemini Code Assist Standard/Enterprise unchanged), the closed-source angle (Gemini CLI Apache 2.0 to Antigravity CLI closed-source Go binary, Issue #27304), how to install agy on macOS/Linux/Windows, a 7-step migration (agy plugin import gemini, .gemini/skills to .agents/skills, MCP url to serverUrl in mcp_config.json, GEMINI.md and AGENTS.md both work), rate-limit shock (1,000 free requests/day to 4-5 turns weekly with 166h reset per Discussion #27274), Antigravity Pro/Ultra credit math, side-by-side vs Claude Code (Opus 4.6, 1M context, 77.2% SWE-bench) and Codex CLI, the paid Gemini API escape hatch, and a 30-day countdown plan.
- [OpenAI Codex Security GitHub Setup Guide: 2026 Walkthrough](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/codex-security-github-setup): End-to-end practitioner walkthrough of OpenAI Codex Security, the AI vulnerability scanner repositioned as the centerpiece of OpenAI's Daybreak cybersecurity initiative (launched May 11 2026). Covers prerequisites (ChatGPT Business/Enterprise/Edu, Codex Cloud environment, GitHub App scopes), the setup flow at chatgpt.com/codex/security, picking a history window, editing the auto-generated threat model with a worked example, triaging Recommended vs All Findings, generating PR patches, accuracy benchmarks (74% TPR on 31 findings vs Semgrep 20% and Snyk 28% on a 162,000-line corpus), side-by-side comparison with Claude Code Security Review, cost math (~$0.02 per 1K LOC, ~$600/month for 20 repos), runtime gaps where DAST still matters (CORS, broken auth, business logic), and troubleshooting (stuck backfill, generic findings, cyber-safety false flags).
- [Claude Managed Agents Outcomes: Auto-Grading Agent Work](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-managed-agents-outcomes): Practitioner guide to Claude Managed Agents Outcomes, the public-beta feature launched May 6 2026 that lets a separate grader auto-evaluate each writer draft against a markdown rubric and feed gaps back as natural-language revision notes. Covers the user.define_outcome event, rubric anti-patterns (vague criteria, unverifiable factual claims, contradictory descriptions), the five span.outcome_evaluation_end.result states (satisfied, needs_revision, max_iterations_reached, failed, interrupted), max_iterations tuning rule (default 3, max 20, start at 5 for strict rubrics), cost math against the $0.08-per-session-hour clock, and how Outcomes compares to standalone LLM-as-judge tools (Galileo, DeepEval, Langfuse) and OpenAI Codex /goal.
- [Running claude ultrareview in CI/CD: GitHub Actions Guide](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/ultrareview-ci-cd-pipelines): Claude Code 2.1.120 added the claude ultrareview non-interactive CLI subcommand on April 28, 2026. Practitioner guide covering the Claude.ai OAuth token gotcha (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY does not work), a copy-pasteable GitHub Actions workflow, --json parsing with jq, cost controls, GitLab CI equivalent, comparison vs the managed Code Review product, and the June 1 2026 Copilot Actions-minutes billing change.
- [MCP Code Execution Pattern: A Hands-On Claude Code Guide](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/mcp-code-execution-pattern): Practitioner guide to the MCP code execution pattern. Three MCP servers can eat 72% of a 200K context window; code mode collapses 2,500 endpoints into ~1,000 tokens. Code execution vs dynamic toolsets vs deferred loading, with a working Python search/execute server and Claude Code v2.1.121 alwaysLoad configuration.
- [Regression-Proof Claude Code Workflows: Pin, Lock, Test](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/regression-proofing-claude-code-workflows): Practitioner playbook after Anthropic's April 23 2026 postmortem. Pin the CLI to v2.1.117, lock effortLevel, allowlist models with availableModels and modelOverrides, add a regression-detecting Stop hook, and keep a fixture suite.
- [Harden Claude Code GitHub Actions: Prompt Injection Defense](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/hardening-ai-agents-cicd-prompt-injection): Defend Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and GitHub Copilot Agent in GitHub Actions against the April 2026 Comment and Control CVE. Tool allowlists, OIDC via Bedrock, script caps, harden-runner egress blocks, and a before/after hardened workflow.
- [Claude Code Security Review GitHub Action: 2026 Setup Guide](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-code-security-review-github-actions): Set up Anthropic's Claude Code Security Review GitHub Action: workflow YAML, false positive tuning, cost math, and comparison with Semgrep and Snyk.
- [Getting Started with the ant CLI: Deploy Claude Agents](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/ant-cli-getting-started): Install the ant CLI, create your first managed agent, and deploy it in under 10 minutes. YAML version control, scripting patterns, and CI/CD.
- [Claude Code Cost Tracking: Monitor and Cut Your Spending](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-code-cost-tracking): Track Claude Code costs with built-in commands, JSONL logs, ccusage, and 7 tips to cut spending by 50%.
- [Claude Managed Agents vs Agent SDK: Which Should You Use?](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-managed-agents): Decision framework for Anthropic's new Claude Managed Agents beta vs the Agent SDK. Covers pricing, MCP integration, and when to pick each.
- [Claude Mythos: What It Means for Developers Who Can't Use It](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-mythos-preview): Analysis of Anthropic's restricted Claude Mythos Preview release, Project Glasswing, benchmarks vs Opus 4.6, and practical guidance for developers.
- [Gemma 4 Models: Which One Should You Actually Use?](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/gemma-4-models-guide): Tested all 4 Gemma 4 model sizes locally. Practical guide to picking E2B, E4B, 26B MoE, or 31B Dense based on hardware and use case.
- [How I Write CLAUDE.md Files That Actually Work (2026)](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/claude-md-guide): Real production CLAUDE.md examples from a Next.js site. Covers anatomy, 7 common mistakes, and when to use hooks vs CLAUDE.md vs custom commands.
- [Complete Guide to Clawdbot: Your Personal AI Assistant](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/clawdbot-guide): How to install and configure Clawdbot, the open-source personal AI assistant that works on WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, and more.
- [Complete Guide to Method CRM MCP Server](https://avinashsangle.com/blog/method-crm-mcp): How to set up and use the Method CRM MCP Server to enable AI assistants like Claude to interact with CRM data.

## Projects
- [Reddit Agent](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/reddit-agent): AI agent that automates Reddit interactions using Claude and MCP.
- [Social Media Auto Poster](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/social-media-auto-poster): Automated social media posting system powered by AI.
- [Jenkins MCP Server](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/jenkins-mcp): Model Context Protocol server for Jenkins CI/CD integration with AI assistants.
- [Jenkins Chatbot](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/jenkins-chatbot): AI-powered chatbot for Jenkins pipeline management and automation.
- [MCP Calculator Server](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/calculator-server): Example MCP server implementation demonstrating the Model Context Protocol.
- [WordPress MCP Server](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/wp-mcp): MCP server enabling AI assistants to manage WordPress sites via natural language.
- [AWS EC2 Agent](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/aws-ec2-agent): AI agent for managing AWS EC2 instances through conversational interfaces.
- [Twitter OAuth](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/twitter-oauth): OAuth implementation for Twitter/X API integration.
- [Method CRM MCP Server](https://avinashsangle.com/projects/method-crm-mcp): MCP server for Method CRM enabling AI-powered CRM interactions.
